**Saluzzo** (Italian: [[saˈluttso]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language"): *Salusse* [[saˈlyse]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Piedmontese "Help:IPA/Piedmontese")) is a town and former principality in the [province of Cuneo](/wiki/Province_of_Cuneo "Province of Cuneo"), in the [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") region, [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy").

The city of Saluzzo is built on a hill overlooking a vast, well-cultivated plain. Iron, lead, silver, marble, slate etc. are found in the surrounding mountains. On 1 January 2017 it had a population of 17.581

Saluzzo was the birthplace of the writer [Silvio Pellico](/wiki/Silvio_Pellico "Silvio Pellico") and of typographer [Giambattista Bodoni](/wiki/Giambattista_Bodoni "Giambattista Bodoni").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saluzzo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Saluzzo_medieval_buildings_11-12-21.jpg/250px-Saluzzo_medieval_buildings_11-12-21.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saluzzo_medieval_buildings_11-12-21.jpg)

Medieval buildings in the old sector

See also: [Marquisate of Saluzzo](/wiki/Marquisate_of_Saluzzo "Marquisate of Saluzzo")

Saluzzo (Salusse in [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language")) was a *civitas* (tribal city state) of the Vagienni, or mountain [Ligures](/wiki/Ligures "Ligures"), and later of the [Salluvii](/wiki/Salluvii "Salluvii"). This district was brought under Roman control by the Consul [Marcus Fulvius](/wiki/Marcus_Fulvius_Flaccus_(consul_125_BC) "Marcus Fulvius Flaccus (consul 125 BC)") c. 125 BC.

In the [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian "Carolingian") age it became the residence of a [count](/wiki/Count "Count"); later, having passed to the Marquesses of [Susa](/wiki/Susa,_Italy "Susa, Italy"), [Manfred I](/wiki/Manfredo_I_of_Saluzzo "Manfredo I of Saluzzo"), son of Marquess Bonifacio del Vasto, on the division of that principality became Marquess of Saluzzo; this family held the marquisate of Saluzzo from 1142 to 1548. The marquisate embraced the territory lying between the Alps, the Po and the Stura, and was extended on several occasions. In the Middle Ages it had a chequered existence, often being in conflict with powerful neighbours, chiefly the Counts (later Dukes) of Savoy. After [Manfred II](/wiki/Manfredo_II_of_Saluzzo "Manfredo II of Saluzzo")'s death, his widow had to accept a series of tributes, which were to be later the base of the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy")'s claims over the increasingly feebler marquises' territories. [Thomas III](/wiki/Thomas_III_of_Saluzzo "Thomas III of Saluzzo"), a vassal of France, wrote the romance *Le chevalier errant* ('the knight-errant').

[Ludovico I](/wiki/Ludovico_I_of_Saluzzo "Ludovico I of Saluzzo") (1416–75) started the Golden Age of the city and imposed himself as a mediator between the neighbouring powers. [Ludovico II](/wiki/Ludovico_II_of_Saluzzo "Ludovico II of Saluzzo") constructed a [tunnel](/wiki/Monte_Viso_Tunnel "Monte Viso Tunnel"), no longer in use, through the [Monviso](/wiki/Monviso "Monviso"), a remarkable work for the time. With the help of the French he resisted a vigorous siege by the Duke of Savoy in 1486, but in 1487 yielded and retired to France where he wrote *L'art de la chevalerie sous Vegèce* ("The art of chivalry under Vegetius", 1488), a treatise on good government, and other works on military affairs. He was a [patron](/wiki/Patron "Patron") of clerics and authors. In 1490 Ludovico regained power, but after his death, his sons struggled longly for the rule and impoverished the state.

After long struggles for independence, the marquisate was occupied (1548) by the French, as a fief of the Crown of [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") – with the name of ***Saluces*** – and remained part of that kingdom until it was ceded to [Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy") in 1601. In 1588 [Charles Emmanuel I of Savoy](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_I_of_Savoy "Charles Emmanuel I of Savoy") took possession of the city. Thenceforward Saluzzo shared the destinies of Piedmont, with which it formed "one of the keys of the house" of Italy.

The Marquisate of Saluzzo is the setting of [Boccaccio's](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") tale of [Griselda](/wiki/Griselda_(folklore) "Griselda (folklore)"), the final story in the [Decameron](/wiki/Decameron "Decameron"), as well as [Chaucer's](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer") [Clerk's Tale](/wiki/The_Clerk%27s_Prologue_and_Tale "The Clerk's Prologue and Tale") in *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The municipality of Saluzzo absorbed at the beginning of 2019 the neighbouring commune of [Castellar](/wiki/Castellar,_Piedmont "Castellar, Piedmont"), thus enforcing the results of a referendum held in the summer of 2018.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saluzzo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

The municipality of Saluces occupies a vast area of 7,659 ha (18,930 acres) in the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley"), about 35 km (22 mi) east of [Mount Viso](/wiki/Monte_Viso "Monte Viso").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Saluzzo_street_Monviso_11-12-21.jpg/250px-Saluzzo_street_Monviso_11-12-21.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saluzzo_street_Monviso_11-12-21.jpg)

Medieval street with the city wall and Monviso visible in the distance

## Duomo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saluzzo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Duomo")]

The [Cathedral of Saluzzo](/w/index.php?title=Cathedral_of_Saluzzo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cathedral of Saluzzo (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Duomo_di_Saluzzo "it:Duomo di Saluzzo")], also known as the Cathedral, dedicated to the Virgin Mary of the Assumption, stands out for its [Late-Gothic](/w/index.php?title=Tardo_gotico&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tardo gotico (page does not exist)") forms; built outside the walls just beyond Porta Santa Maria between 1491 and 1501, it was a bishop's seat starting from 1511. The façade is in exposed brick, adorned by three portals surmounted by terracotta gables that house statues of the apostles (central portal), while above the side there are the patron San Chiaffredo and San Costanzo. The interior has a covering made up of cross vaults, while the Baroque high altar with its large impact is of great impact eleven wooden statues by [Carlo Giuseppe Plura](/wiki/Carlo_Giuseppe_Plura "Carlo Giuseppe Plura") and collaborators. In the central nave, you can admire a precious fourteenth-century wooden crucifix. To the left of the main altar is the Chapel of the SS. Sacramento, with a polyptych by the Flemish artist of French origin [Hans Clemer](/wiki/Hans_Clemer "Hans Clemer"), better known as Maestro d'Elva.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saluzzo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The [Marquisate of Saluzzo](/wiki/Marquisate_of_Saluzzo "Marquisate of Saluzzo") was the seat of a [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_Republic "Piedmontese Republic") principality whose history is closely linked to that of its powerful neighbor, the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), until its definitive incorporation obtained in 1601 by Duke [Charles Emmanuel](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_I,_Duke_of_Savoy "Charles Emmanuel I, Duke of Savoy").

The French name of Saluces was given to the city during the period of French domination. There are many traces of this francization still today as on the pediment of the casa cavassa today transformed into a museum where you can read the motto "right whatever it is."

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saluzzo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Main sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Saluzzo0002.jpg/250px-Saluzzo0002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saluzzo0002.jpg)

Chiesa di San Giovanni

* [Cathedral of Saluzzo](/w/index.php?title=Cathedral_of_Saluzzo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cathedral of Saluzzo (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Duomo_di_Saluzzo "it:Duomo di Saluzzo")] (1491–1501): Church was built in Lombard-Gothic style. The façade is decorated with rose-windows, frescoes (almost totally faded) and statues. The interior contains a magnificent Baroque high altar, plus a 16th-century terracotta group portraying the *Deposition*, ad *Adoration of the Shepherds* by [Sebastiano Ricci](/wiki/Sebastiano_Ricci "Sebastiano Ricci").
* *San Bernardo* church formerly belonging to the Conventuals, has interesting tombs of the counts della Torre.
* *San Giovanni*: Dominican church begun in 1330 in Gothic style and completed in 1504 with [Bramantesque](/wiki/Bramante "Bramante") influences. The façade is [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"). The apse, from 1504, houses a Funerary Chapel of Ludovico II, work by [Antoine Le Moiturier](/wiki/Antoine_Le_Moiturier "Antoine Le Moiturier") (1425–74) covered with green stone and characterized by an elegant play of arches and sculptures. The sepulchre of Ludovico II is a Renaissance work by [Benedetto Briosco](/wiki/Benedetto_Briosco "Benedetto Briosco"). Annexed are the Gothic cloister and the Capitular Hall with the Mausoleum of a vicar-general of the marquisate, Galeazzo Cavassa di Carmagnola, a Renaissance work by [Matteo Sanmicheli](/w/index.php?title=Matteo_Sanmicheli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matteo Sanmicheli (page does not exist)").
* [Casa Cavassa](/wiki/Casa_Cavassa "Casa Cavassa"): site of the civic museum and rebuilt with a Renaissance interior.
* *San Agostino* and *San Bernardino*
* *Town Hall* is the former [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") College, while the older one (1462), with a bold tower, is utilized by the Court of Assizes. The 16th century *Villa Belvedere* has elegant rooms with late-Renaissance decorations.

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saluzzo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notable people")]

* [Alice of Saluzzo](/wiki/Alice_of_Saluzzo "Alice of Saluzzo")
* General [Carlo Alberto Dalla Chiesa](/wiki/Carlo_Alberto_Dalla_Chiesa "Carlo Alberto Dalla Chiesa")
* Marquess [Michele Antonio di Saluzzo](/wiki/Michele_Antonio_di_Saluzzo "Michele Antonio di Saluzzo")
* [Silvio Pellico](/wiki/Silvio_Pellico "Silvio Pellico"), writer
* [Giambattista Bodoni](/wiki/Giambattista_Bodoni "Giambattista Bodoni"), typographer
* [Corrado Segre](/wiki/Corrado_Segre "Corrado Segre"), mathematician
* [Federico Lombardi](/wiki/Federico_Lombardi "Federico Lombardi"), Jesuit priest, director of [Radio Vatican](/wiki/Radio_Vatican "Radio Vatican")
* [Pier Paolo Maggiora](/wiki/Pier_Paolo_Maggiora "Pier Paolo Maggiora"), architect
* [Luciano Nizzola](/w/index.php?title=Luciano_Nizzola&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luciano Nizzola (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luciano_Nizzola "it:Luciano Nizzola")], soccer dirigent
* [Paolo De Chiesa](/wiki/Paolo_De_Chiesa "Paolo De Chiesa"), skier
* [Magda Olivero](/wiki/Magda_Olivero "Magda Olivero"), singer
* [Isacco Levi](/w/index.php?title=Isacco_Levi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Isacco Levi (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isacco_Levi "it:Isacco Levi")], partisan
* [Giovanni Canaveri](/wiki/Juan_de_Canaveris "Juan de Canaveris"), related to the [May Revolution](/wiki/May_Revolution "May Revolution")
* [Matteo Olivero](/wiki/Matteo_Olivero "Matteo Olivero"), Italian painter related to [divisionism](/wiki/Divisionism "Divisionism")
* [Alberto Basso](/wiki/Alberto_Basso "Alberto Basso"), music historian and lexicographer
* [Giacomo Segre](/wiki/Giacomo_Segre "Giacomo Segre"), artillery officer in the [Third Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Third_Italian_War_of_Independence "Third Italian War of Independence"), born in Saluzzo
* [Elvira Petrozzi](/wiki/Elvira_Petrozzi "Elvira Petrozzi"), [nun](/wiki/Nun#Catholicism "Nun") and founder of Saluzzo-based international youth community Comunità Cenacolo[[5]](#cite_note-cenacolo-laityfamilylife-5)[[6]](#cite_note-cenacolo-chi-siamo-it-6)
